response:
When developing a cross-platform application that uses WPF on Windows and Cocoa on Mac, you're encountering an issue with the `PresentationFramework` assembly not being found on macOS. This is because the WPF UI is dynamically linked to the main .NET 7 application, which is compiled for Windows. On macOS, the WPF framework isn't natively supported, so the assembly isn't available, leading to the `FileNotFoundException`.

To resolve this, you need to ensure that the WPF UI is properly isolated from the main application. One approach is to use a separate WPF project that is compiled for Windows, and then dynamically load its UI into the macOS version of the application. This way, the WPF assembly is only present on Windows, and the macOS version doesn't attempt to load it.

The error occurs because the macOS version of the application is trying to load the `PresentationFramework` assembly, which is not available on macOS. This is a common issue when using WPF in a cross-platform setup, as the WPF framework is not natively supported on macOS.

To solve this, you can use a technique called "assembly binding redirection" or "dynamic assembly loading" to ensure that the correct version of the WPF assembly is loaded. This involves using the `Assembly.Load` method or the `Assembly.LoadFrom` method to load the WPF assembly dynamically, depending on the platform.

Cousin to this issue is the problem of ensuring that the WPF UI is not compiled into the macOS version of the application. This requires careful management of the project structure and build configurations to ensure that the WPF project is only compiled for Windows and not for macOS.

Finally, you should test your application on both Windows and macOS to ensure that the WPF UI is properly loaded and that the `PresentationFramework` assembly is only available on Windows. This will help you avoid the `FileNotFoundException` and ensure a smooth cross-platform experience.
